Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

John Searle

b. 1932

Biological naturalism, intentionality, and the Chinese Room.

John Searle’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a sustained attempt to defend a realist, biological, anti-reductionist, anti-dualist account of mind. His central doctrine is usually called biological naturalism: conscious and intentional mental phenomena are real features of the natural world; they are caused by neurobiological processes and realized in the brain; but they cannot be eliminated, behavioristically translated, or fully captured by third-person functional/computational descriptions. Searle’s mature view treats consciousness, intentionality, perception, action, language, rationality, and social reality as parts of one continuous philosophical architecture. He is not merely offering an argument against artificial intelligence, although that is what made him famous outside philosophy. He is trying to rebuild the philosophy of mind around the biological reality of subjective consciousness.

Searle’s position is historically important because it sits at a crossroads. He inherits the ordinary-language tradition of J. L. Austin and the speech-act theory tradition, enters the philosophy of mind through intentionality and language, attacks the computational theory of mind at the height of classical AI optimism, and becomes one of the major analytic philosophers insisting that consciousness must be treated as a central philosophical and scientific datum rather than a marginal embarrassment. The broader field had moved from behaviorism to identity theory, then to functionalism and computationalism; Searle’s intervention is a rejection of all four when they deny, reduce, or mislocate consciousness and intrinsic intentionality.

1. Historical placement in philosophy of mind

The background against which Searle should be read is the twentieth-century attempt to naturalize mind. Early and mid-century behaviorism sought to make psychology scientifically respectable by focusing on behavior and excluding private mental states from the proper domain of empirical inquiry. In philosophy, logical behaviorism tried to analyze mental-state talk in terms of actual and possible behavior. In psychology, methodological behaviorism made consciousness and introspective reports suspect as scientific data.

The next major move was mind-brain identity theory, associated with figures such as U. T. Place and Herbert Feigl in the 1950s. Identity theorists held that mental states, especially sensations and experiences, are identical with brain processes. This was a more straightforwardly physicalist view than behaviorism: pain is not a disposition to behave; pain is a neural process. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes the identity theory as the view that experiences “just are” brain processes, a materialist thesis that emerged in the 1950s.

Functionalism then largely displaced identity theory. On the functionalist view, a mental state is defined not by the biological stuff that realizes it but by its causal role: its relations to sensory inputs, other mental states, and behavioral outputs. Pain, for example, is whatever state is typically caused by bodily damage, causes aversive behavior, interacts with beliefs and desires, and so on. This was attractive because it allowed for multiple realizability: creatures with very different physical constitutions, and perhaps machines, could have the same mental states if they implemented the same functional organization. Functionalism became one of the dominant approaches in the last third of the twentieth century.

The cognitive revolution added another layer. After behaviorism’s dominance through the 1950s, cognitive science reintroduced mental representations, information processing, computational modeling, and internal procedures into the scientific study of mind. Classical artificial intelligence and the computational theory of mind encouraged the thought that minds are, at the relevant level of abstraction, computational systems. Hilary Putnam’s machine functionalism and later computational theories treated mental activity as symbol manipulation governed by rules.

Searle’s philosophy of mind is a reaction against this trajectory, but not a nostalgic return to Cartesianism. He rejects behaviorism because it leaves out the subject matter: conscious experience. He rejects reductive identity theory because it neglects the first-person ontology of consciousness. He rejects orthodox functionalism because functional role is not sufficient for genuine mental content. He rejects strong computationalism because syntax, computation, and formal symbol manipulation do not by themselves produce semantics, understanding, or intrinsic intentionality. Yet he also rejects substance dualism. His aim is to preserve the reality of mind while placing it fully inside the biological world.

2. Searle’s master thesis: biological naturalism

Searle’s comprehensive view can be stated in one sentence: conscious mental phenomena are higher-level biological features of the brain, caused by lower-level neurobiological processes, ontologically subjective, and nevertheless perfectly real parts of the objective natural world. This is the core of biological naturalism. In his own framing, consciousness is not a separate substance, not a ghostly nonphysical property, not a behavioral pattern, and not a mere computational role. It is a biological phenomenon, like digestion or growth, though with the distinctive feature that its mode of existence is first-personal.

The key distinction is between ontological subjectivity and epistemic subjectivity. Something is epistemically subjective when its truth is merely a matter of opinion or taste, as in “this painting is beautiful.” Something is epistemically objective when its truth can be settled independently of anyone’s preferences, as in “the painting weighs five kilograms.” But something is ontologically subjective when it exists only as experienced by a subject. Pain, visual experience, anxiety, and conscious thought are ontologically subjective because their existence depends on being experienced. Searle’s important claim is that ontologically subjective phenomena can be studied epistemically objectively. A science of consciousness is not ruled out by the fact that consciousness has a first-person mode of existence.

This gives Searle a distinctive position in the mind-body debate. He thinks the traditional opposition between “mental” and “physical” is a philosophical trap. If “physical” means “part of the natural causal order,” then consciousness is physical. If “mental” means “subjective, qualitative, intentional, or conscious,” then consciousness is mental. The terms are not mutually exclusive. The problem, on Searle’s diagnosis, is that philosophers inherited a bad vocabulary from Cartesian dualism and then tried to solve the mind-body problem within the assumptions that created it. His remedy is to say: there is one world; in that world, brains produce consciousness; consciousness is a real biological feature; and its subjectivity does not make it supernatural.

Biological naturalism has four commitments that define the whole system.

First, consciousness is causally dependent on brain processes. There is no free-floating mental realm.

Second, consciousness is realized in the brain. It is not an illusion, a linguistic convenience, or an external behavior pattern.

Third, consciousness is ontologically irreducible in a special sense. A complete third-person neurobiological description does not replace the first-person existence of the experience. Explaining the neural causes of pain does not show that pain never existed.

Fourth, consciousness is causally efficacious. Searle is not an epiphenomenalist. Conscious intentions, perceptions, decisions, and experiences are not idle accompaniments to real physical processes; they are real features of the functioning organism.

The most delicate part of this doctrine is the relation between causal reducibility and ontological irreducibility. Searle accepts that consciousness may be causally explained by neurobiology. But he denies that causal explanation entails ontological elimination. For many phenomena, to explain the underlying causes is also to reduce the phenomenon ontologically. For example, liquidity can be explained by molecular behavior, and after the explanation there is no extra liquid-substance over and above the molecules. Searle thinks consciousness is different because the phenomenon to be explained includes its subjective mode of appearance. A reduction that leaves out the subjective experience has not reduced consciousness; it has changed the subject.

3. Consciousness: qualitative, subjective, unified

For Searle, consciousness is the central problem of the philosophy of mind. He defines it broadly as the field of subjective awareness that begins when one wakes from dreamless sleep and continues through waking life, including perceptions, bodily sensations, moods, emotions, thoughts, memories, and intentions. Consciousness is not one mental phenomenon among others; it is the condition under which many mental phenomena have their primary form.

Searle identifies three fundamental features of consciousness: qualitativeness, subjectivity, and unity. Qualitativeness means that conscious states have a specific “what it is like” character: the smell of coffee, the look of red, the feel of pain, the tone of embarrassment. Subjectivity means that conscious states exist only from the point of view of a subject. Unity means that consciousness normally comes as a unified field, not as isolated atomic bits. One does not first have a bare color patch, then a bare sound, then a bare bodily sensation, and then somehow add them together. One has a unified conscious scene in which many elements are differentiated.

This leads Searle to reject what he calls a “building-block” model of consciousness. On that model, consciousness is assembled out of small conscious units, as if the mind were a heap of discrete sensory atoms. Searle instead emphasizes the unified field: particular experiences occur within a background field of consciousness. The unity is not merely an additional relation among already self-standing conscious fragments; it is a structural feature of conscious life itself.

His treatment of consciousness is also meant to correct a tendency in both philosophy and psychology to treat third-person observability as the mark of reality. Searle’s response is that consciousness is not less real because it is directly accessible only from the first-person point of view. The pain is real even though only the sufferer can feel it. Others can observe behavior, neural correlates, verbal reports, and causal conditions, but the pain itself exists as experienced. For Searle, denying this is not scientific hardheadedness; it is a refusal to acknowledge the phenomenon that needs explaining.

4. Intentionality: the mind’s directedness toward the world

The second major pillar of Searle’s philosophy of mind is intentionality, the “aboutness” or directedness of mental states. Beliefs are about how things are; desires are about how one wants things to be; fears are about what one fears; perceptions are of objects and events; intentions are directed toward actions. Searle’s 1983 book Intentionality: An Essay in the Philosophy of Mind treats philosophy of language as dependent on philosophy of mind, because linguistic meaning is possible only because speakers already have intentional mental states.

A central concept in Searle’s theory is the condition of satisfaction. An intentional state represents the world as being a certain way, or as to be made a certain way, and it is satisfied if reality matches its content in the appropriate manner. A belief that it is raining is satisfied if it is raining. A desire for water is satisfied if one gets water. An intention to raise one’s arm is satisfied if one raises one’s arm in the right way.

Searle also uses the idea of direction of fit. Beliefs have a mind-to-world direction of fit: the belief is supposed to fit the world. Desires and intentions have a world-to-mind direction of fit: the world is supposed to be changed to match the content of the desire or intention. Perception and action have more complex structures. In perception, the mind is answerable to the world, but the experience also involves causal dependence on the object perceived. In action, the intentional state is supposed to produce the bodily movement that satisfies it.

Another crucial Searlean idea is aspectual shape. Intentional states do not merely point at objects; they represent objects under aspects. One can believe that “the morning star is bright” without believing that “the evening star is bright,” even if both expressions refer to Venus. One can desire water under the aspect of something drinkable without desiring H₂O under a chemical description. This matters because intentionality is not just causal covariance between brain states and worldly items. It involves the way the object or state of affairs is presented to the subject.

Searle’s most important distinction here is between intrinsic intentionality and derived intentionality. Human and animal mental states have intrinsic intentionality: beliefs, desires, perceptions, and intentions are genuinely about things. Words, maps, diagrams, computer symbols, traffic signs, and formal inscriptions have derived intentionality: they mean what they mean only because users with minds assign or sustain that meaning. The intentionality of language is therefore not basic; it derives from the intentionality of speakers.

This is why Searle sees philosophy of language as a branch of philosophy of mind. His early work on speech acts is not abandoned in his later philosophy of mind; it is integrated into it. Speech acts such as promising, asserting, ordering, apologizing, and requesting are forms of intentional action. They work because speakers have beliefs, intentions, commitments, and capacities for rule-governed communication. Language is a public, socially structured extension of more basic biological intentionality.

5. The Network and the Background

Searle denies that intentional states are isolated atoms. Any particular belief, desire, perception, or intention functions only within a Network of other intentional states. For example, an intention to run for president presupposes a vast network of beliefs about elections, citizenship, offices, campaigns, political institutions, other people, time, money, law, and one’s own identity. The content of a single intentional state depends on its relations to many others.

But even the Network is not enough. Searle also argues for what he calls the Background: the set of nonrepresentational capacities, skills, habits, practical know-how, bodily abilities, and taken-for-granted assumptions that make intentional states possible. The Background is not itself a set of beliefs or rules. It is not an unconscious theory inside the head. It is the practical know-how that allows one to interpret language, recognize situations, use objects, move through social space, and apply concepts appropriately.

The Background is one of Searle’s most important contributions to the philosophy of psychology because it resists an overly intellectualized view of the mind. Human beings do not navigate the world by explicitly consulting rules for every action. We know how to sit in chairs, open doors, stand at appropriate distances, understand jokes, read facial expressions, and use tools without representing every condition of correct performance. Intentionality is therefore embedded in a bodily, social, and practical field of competence.

This doctrine also marks a partial convergence with phenomenology and later embodied-cognition approaches, even though Searle remains an analytic philosopher and a biological naturalist. Like Heidegger or Merleau-Ponty, he insists that explicit representation rests on a more basic practical familiarity with the world. But unlike many phenomenologists, he keeps this point within a naturalistic theory of brain, action, and intentionality.

6. Perception: direct realism and presentational intentionality

Searle’s theory of perception is a direct realist theory. He rejects the idea that we directly perceive inner sense-data, mental images, or private intermediaries, and only indirectly infer the external world. In normal perception, we see tables, trees, people, colors, shapes, and events in the world. The subjective visual experience is not the object seen; it is the experiencing through which worldly objects are presented.

In Seeing Things as They Are, Searle develops an account of perceptual intentionality in which perceptual experience has conditions of satisfaction. A visual experience presents the world as being a certain way, and it is veridical if the world is that way and if the experience is caused by the relevant objects or conditions in the right manner. This makes perceptual content partly self-referential: the experience is satisfied only if the perceived state of affairs causes that very experience in the appropriate way.

Searle calls perception a form of presentational intentionality. In belief or imagination, an object may be represented as absent or merely possible. In perception, the object is experienced as directly present. This presentational character distinguishes perception from thought, memory, and fantasy. Perception is not a neutral internal representation later interpreted as being about the world; it is already an experience of the world as there.

Illusion and hallucination do not refute direct realism for Searle. They show that perceptual experience can have conditions of satisfaction that are not met. In hallucination, there is a subjective experience with intentional content, but no corresponding object causing the experience in the required way. The error is not that we always perceive inner objects; the error is that, in abnormal cases, the experience presents the world as containing something that is not there.

This view is continuous with Searle’s general philosophy of mind. Perceptual consciousness is subjective and qualitative, but it is also intentionally directed toward the world. It is biologically produced, but its content is not reducible to bare neural firing. It has aspectual shape, conditions of satisfaction, causal structure, and a place within the unified field of consciousness.

7. The unconscious and Searle’s “Connection Principle”

Searle’s view of the unconscious is more restrictive than many psychoanalytic, computational, and cognitive-scientific accounts. He does not deny that there are nonconscious brain processes. On the contrary, biological naturalism requires them. What he denies is that every subpersonal information-processing state should automatically count as a genuinely intentional mental state.

His Connection Principle says that an unconscious intentional state must be, in principle, accessible to consciousness. If a state is genuinely an unconscious belief, desire, fear, or intention, then it must have the kind of aspectual intentional content that could become conscious, even if it is not conscious now. Otherwise, it is not literally an unconscious intentional state; it is a nonconscious neurophysiological cause or disposition. Searle’s view is that unconscious intentional states are mental because of their causal capacity to produce conscious intentional states.

This has large consequences for psychology. Searle rejects the inflationary tendency to treat any computationally describable process as a mental representation. A thermostat does not literally believe the room is cold. A visual-processing subsystem does not necessarily have unconscious beliefs in the same sense a person has a belief. A neural mechanism may process information, but information processing is not automatically intentionality in the full mental sense. For Searle, psychology must distinguish between genuinely mental states, dispositional unconscious states, subpersonal neural mechanisms, and as-if intentional descriptions used for explanatory convenience.

This is one of the points at which Searle most strongly opposes computational cognitive science. He accepts that computational models can be useful tools, but he denies that the formal model is itself the mind. The psychological reality lies in conscious and potentially conscious intentional states produced by a biological organism, not in the abstract syntax of a program.

8. The Chinese Room and the critique of strong AI

Searle’s most famous argument is the Chinese Room argument, first published in 1980. It targets what he calls strong AI: the thesis that the right computer program is not merely a tool for simulating understanding but literally has understanding. Searle distinguishes this from weak AI, the more modest and acceptable thesis that computers are useful instruments for modeling, testing, and assisting psychological explanation.

The thought experiment is familiar. Imagine a person locked in a room who does not understand Chinese. Chinese symbols are passed into the room. The person follows an English rulebook for manipulating those symbols and passes Chinese symbols back out. From outside, the room may appear to understand Chinese because its outputs are appropriate. But the person inside does not understand Chinese; they are only manipulating symbols formally. Searle argues that a computer program is likewise formal or syntactic. It manipulates symbols according to rules, but syntax by itself is not semantics. Therefore, running the right program is not sufficient for understanding.

The argument is not that machines can never think. Searle’s claim is more precise: a machine could think only if it had the right causal powers, comparable to those of the brain. A biological brain is a machine in a broad sense. The problem is not mechanism; the problem is the assumption that formal computation alone is enough. Searle’s own formulation says that human and animal intentionality is caused by brain causal features, and that instantiating a program is not sufficient for intentionality.

The Chinese Room is aimed at functionalism and the computational theory of mind as much as at AI. If a mental state is wholly defined by formal input-output relations, then the system in the room should understand Chinese. Searle thinks this conclusion is absurd. The missing element is intrinsic intentionality: the symbols mean nothing to the person manipulating them, and their “meaning” exists only in the interpretation supplied by outside observers.

Several standard replies have been made. The systems reply says that the individual in the room does not understand Chinese, but the whole system does. The robot reply says that if the program were connected to sensors and effectors in the world, it could acquire genuine meaning. The brain simulator reply says that a program simulating the neural activity of a Chinese speaker would understand. Searle’s replies are consistent with his basic thesis: adding more syntax does not create semantics, embodiment alone does not suffice unless the system has the right causal powers, and simulating a brain is not the same as duplicating its causal powers. The debate remains unresolved, and the Chinese Room continues to be one of the central arguments in philosophy of mind and AI.

The deeper point is often missed. Searle is not saying that artificial systems are impossible minds by definition. He is saying that computation is observer-relative. A physical system counts as implementing a computation only relative to an interpretation that assigns formal symbols and transition rules to it. But consciousness and intrinsic intentionality are not observer-relative in that way. My pain does not become pain because someone interprets it as pain. My belief does not become a belief because an observer assigns it a role in a formal model. This is why computation, for Searle, cannot be the ontological ground of mind.

9. Psychology after Searle: what must a science of mind study?

Searle’s philosophy of psychology is an attack on any psychology that eliminates consciousness, treats intentionality as merely metaphorical, or identifies mental explanation with formal computation. He is not anti-science. He is anti-misdescription. A scientific psychology must study the mind as it actually exists: conscious, intentional, biologically embodied, causally efficacious, socially embedded, and dependent on a Background of skills.

This puts Searle in opposition to classical behaviorism, which excluded consciousness from scientific psychology, and also to forms of cognitive science that reintroduced the inner while describing it primarily as symbol manipulation. The cognitive revolution had restored talk of internal representations and computational processes after behaviorism’s dominance, but Searle argues that this still does not recover the intrinsic subjectivity of consciousness or the intrinsic intentionality of mental states.

A Searlean psychology would include at least five domains.

First, it would study conscious experience as a real biological phenomenon, not merely as verbal report or behavioral disposition.

Second, it would study intentionality: how beliefs, desires, perceptions, memories, intentions, emotions, and actions are directed toward objects and states of affairs.

Third, it would study the neurobiological basis of consciousness and intentionality, without assuming that third-person neural descriptions eliminate first-person phenomena.

Fourth, it would study the Background: embodied skills, social competence, practical know-how, and pre-reflective capacities.

Fifth, it would study language and social cognition as extensions of mental intentionality, not as autonomous symbol systems detached from biological minds.

This makes Searle a realist about folk-psychological categories, but not an uncritical defender of common sense. Belief, desire, intention, perception, and pain are not theoretical posits to be eliminated when neuroscience advances. They are part of the data. But their proper explanation requires biology, action theory, and social theory.

10. Action, rationality, and free will

Searle’s theory of action extends his theory of intentionality. He distinguishes between prior intentions and intentions-in-action. A prior intention is formed before the action: I decide tonight that I will go running tomorrow morning. An intention-in-action is the intentional component present during the action itself: I am now running, raising my arm, speaking, or opening the door. Actions are not mere bodily movements; they are bodily movements under intentional descriptions and caused in the right way by intentions.

In Rationality in Action, Searle argues that human agency involves a distinctive experience of the gap. In deliberation, our reasons do not normally function as sufficient causes that mechanically force a decision. We experience a gap between reasons and decision, between prior intention and action, and sometimes between the initiation and completion of action. This is not an argument that free will has been metaphysically solved; it is an analysis of how rational agency is structured from the first-person point of view.

Searle’s view of reasons also opposes simple desire-based models of rationality. He argues for desire-independent reasons. Promises, commitments, obligations, needs, and institutional roles can give an agent reasons to act even when the agent does not antecedently desire the outcome. This connects his philosophy of action with his theory of speech acts and social ontology. A promise creates a commitment; that commitment creates a reason; the reason can rationally guide action.

Free will is one of the less settled areas of Searle’s system. He thinks the experience of freedom is unavoidable in practical deliberation: when deciding what to do, one cannot treat one’s future action simply as an event already fixed in the same way one predicts an eclipse. Yet Searle also wants a naturalistic account compatible with the brain. The result is a tension: he resists compatibilist reductions of freedom to ordinary causation, but he does not posit a Cartesian soul. His likely solution, if there is one, must be neurobiological, but he does not provide a fully worked-out empirical account.

11. Emotions, moods, and the qualitative-intentional structure of mind

Although Searle is less famous for a theory of emotion than for consciousness, intentionality, and AI, his framework implies a rich account of affective life. Emotions often combine qualitative consciousness with intentional content. Fear, anger, shame, pride, grief, and desire are not merely bodily sensations; they are directed toward objects or situations under descriptions. Fear represents something as threatening. Anger represents something as offensive or unjust. Shame represents oneself as exposed, diminished, or normatively compromised.

This makes emotions important test cases for Searle’s system. They are qualitative, subjective, biologically realized, and intentionally directed. A purely behaviorist theory misses their felt character. A purely computational theory risks missing their first-person qualitative force. A purely neurophysiological account may identify causal mechanisms but still must explain what the emotion is like and what it is about.

Moods are more complicated because they may lack a determinate object. Anxiety, melancholy, irritation, or elation can color the entire conscious field without being clearly about one object. Searle’s unified-field model of consciousness can handle this better than atomistic models: a mood can be a modification of the whole conscious field rather than a discrete representation with a sharply defined propositional content.

12. Language, speech acts, and the mind

Searle’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his philosophy of language. His early work on speech acts argued that language is a form of rule-governed action. To assert, promise, command, request, apologize, or declare is not merely to emit sounds; it is to perform an intentional act under conditions of success or failure. His later theory of intentionality gives this view a deeper foundation: speech acts have meaning because speakers have mental intentionality.

The relation is asymmetric. Language depends on mind, but mind does not depend on language in the same way. Prelinguistic children and nonhuman animals can have perceptions, desires, pains, fears, and intentions. But public linguistic meaning requires the biological intentionality of speakers and hearers. Words do not intrinsically mean anything. They have meaning because intentional agents use them in rule-governed social practices.

Searle’s speech-act theory also helps explain his later social ontology. Institutional facts—money, marriage, property, universities, governments, legal rights—exist because human beings collectively assign functions and statuses. These social facts are objective in the sense that they can be publicly true or false, but they depend on collective intentionality. Searle denies that collective intentionality requires a group mind; collective intentions exist in individual brains, though they are not reducible to merely private individual intentions.

This matters for philosophy of mind because it shows that Searle’s account is not narrowly individualistic in the way some biological theories are. The biological mind is the foundation, but human mental life is socially extended through language, institutions, commitments, and shared practices.

13. Searle’s position against major alternatives

Against Cartesian dualism, Searle argues that there are not two substances, mind and matter. Consciousness is not outside nature. It is a biological feature of organisms. The dualist is right that consciousness is subjective, but wrong to infer that it belongs to a separate metaphysical realm.

Against reductive materialism, Searle argues that consciousness cannot be eliminated or reduced to third-person descriptions without losing the phenomenon. The reductive materialist is right that consciousness depends on the brain, but wrong to infer that subjective experience is unreal, merely linguistic, or identical with an objective description that omits subjectivity.

Against behaviorism, Searle argues that behavior is evidence of mind but not identical with mind. Pain behavior is not pain. Linguistic behavior is not understanding. The inner conscious and intentional phenomena are what the theory must explain, not what it may define away.

Against identity theory, Searle agrees that mental phenomena are brain phenomena, but denies that this solves the problem if identity is understood in a way that removes first-person ontology. Consciousness is realized in the brain, but its subjective mode of existence remains central.

Against functionalism, Searle argues that causal role is insufficient for mental content. A system could instantiate the relevant formal relations without understanding anything, as the Chinese Room is meant to show. Functional organization may be part of the story, but it is not enough.

Against computationalism, Searle argues that computation is syntactic and observer-relative, whereas consciousness and intrinsic intentionality are semantic and not observer-relative. Computers can simulate mental processes, assist psychology, and perhaps be components in systems with mental causal powers, but a program as such is not a mind.

Against eliminative materialism, Searle argues that eliminating consciousness is self-defeating or absurd. Any theory that says consciousness does not really exist is less certain than the fact that conscious experience exists. For Searle, the existence of consciousness is not a theoretical hypothesis; it is the starting point.

14. Strengths of Searle’s philosophy of mind

Searle’s greatest strength is that he refuses to let theory erase the phenomenon. He insists that consciousness is real, qualitative, subjective, unified, and biologically grounded. This gives his philosophy a strong phenomenological anchor without abandoning naturalism.

A second strength is the distinction between ontological and epistemic subjectivity. This distinction dissolves a common confusion: the idea that because consciousness is subjective in its mode of existence, it cannot be studied objectively. Searle’s answer is that science can make objective claims about subjective phenomena. That is an important conceptual clarification for psychology, neuroscience, and psychiatry.

A third strength is his integration of consciousness and intentionality. Some philosophers focus on qualia while neglecting intentionality; others focus on representation while neglecting phenomenal consciousness. Searle treats them together. Perception, action, emotion, thought, and language all involve both subjective experience and world-directed content.

A fourth strength is his critique of derived intentionality. Searle makes it difficult to slide casually from “this system manipulates symbols” to “this system understands.” His distinction between intrinsic and derived intentionality remains one of the most important conceptual tools in debates over AI, language models, and artificial consciousness.

A fifth strength is the unity of his broader project. His theories of speech acts, intentionality, consciousness, rationality, perception, and social reality are not separate fragments. They form a systematic picture: biological minds generate intentionality; intentionality underlies language; language enables commitments and institutions; institutions structure reasons for action.

15. Main difficulties and criticisms

The first major difficulty is that Searle’s appeal to biological causal powers can seem underdeveloped. He says the brain has the right causal powers for consciousness and that programs alone do not. But critics ask: what exactly are these causal powers? Are they electrochemical properties, dynamical properties, biological organization, embodiment, metabolism, or something else? Without a more precise account, biological naturalism can look like a promissory note.

The second difficulty is the tension between causal reducibility and ontological irreducibility. Searle says consciousness is caused by and realized in brain processes, but not ontologically reducible to them. Critics argue that this risks collapsing either into property dualism or into ordinary physicalism with special terminology. Searle denies both, but the exact metaphysical status of subjective biological features remains contested.

The third difficulty concerns the Connection Principle. Many cognitive scientists posit unconscious representations that are not in principle conscious in their current form: low-level visual processing, syntactic parsing, motor control, predictive coding, memory consolidation, and other subpersonal processes. Searle can describe these as nonconscious causal mechanisms rather than unconscious intentional states, but critics may see that as too restrictive for empirical psychology.

The fourth difficulty is the Chinese Room’s dependence on intuitions about understanding. Critics of Searle argue that the system as a whole may understand even if the person in the room does not, or that meaning can arise from embodied causal interaction with the world, or that Searle unfairly treats computation as merely formal when real computational systems are physically implemented. Searle’s replies are powerful, but the debate has not produced consensus.

The fifth difficulty concerns perception. Searle’s direct realism avoids sense-data theories, but critics argue that hallucination, illusion, and the common factor between veridical and nonveridical experience require a more complex account than direct realism allows. Reviews of his perceptual theory have pressed him on whether his treatment of “seeing as” and perceptual content can handle the full range of perceptual error.

The sixth difficulty is that Searle sometimes relies on a very strong distinction between syntax and semantics. Contemporary AI, embodied cognition, enactivism, and predictive-processing theories may resist the idea that computation is purely formal in the way Searle assumes. Even so, his challenge remains: any theory of artificial mind must explain how genuine subjective understanding arises, not merely how successful input-output behavior is produced.

16. Extracting a comprehensive Searlean philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Searle can be organized around ten theses.

First, there is one natural world. Searle rejects Cartesian dualism. Mind is not a separate substance or supernatural domain.

Second, consciousness is real. It is not behavior, not a useful fiction, not an illusion, and not a merely verbal construct.

Third, consciousness is biological. It is caused by neurobiological processes and realized in brain structures. The right science for explaining consciousness is ultimately a biological science, not formal computer science alone.

Fourth, consciousness is ontologically subjective. Its existence depends on being experienced by a subject. This is not a defect; it is the phenomenon’s mode of existence.

Fifth, objective science of subjective reality is possible. First-person ontology does not imply epistemic relativism. Neuroscience, psychology, and philosophy can make objective claims about consciousness.

Sixth, intentionality is intrinsic in biological minds. Mental states are genuinely about things. Linguistic and computational symbols have meaning only derivatively, through the intentionality of agents.

Seventh, intentionality has structure. Intentional states have conditions of satisfaction, direction of fit, aspectual shape, and causal relations to perception and action. They are not mere internal labels or causal correlations.

Eighth, intentionality depends on the Network and Background. Particular mental states make sense only within a web of other mental states and a deeper layer of nonrepresentational practical capacities.

Ninth, computation alone is not mind. Formal symbol manipulation may simulate understanding, but it does not by itself produce understanding. A thinking machine would need the causal powers sufficient for consciousness and intentionality, not merely the right program.

Tenth, human mindedness extends into language, rationality, and institutions. Speech acts, promises, obligations, social facts, and collective intentionality are built on biological intentionality but generate higher-order forms of human reality.

17. Final assessment

Searle’s philosophy of mind is best classified as anti-Cartesian biological realism. It is realist because it treats consciousness and intentionality as genuine phenomena. It is biological because it grounds them in the brain. It is anti-reductionist because it denies that third-person functional, behavioral, or computational descriptions exhaust subjective experience. It is anti-dualist because it refuses to place consciousness outside the natural order.

His historical importance lies in the fact that he challenged the dominant late-twentieth-century assumption that mind could be understood primarily through behavior, identity, functional role, or computation. He helped force analytic philosophy of mind back toward consciousness at a time when many theories treated consciousness as secondary. He also gave one of the most influential criticisms of strong AI, a criticism that remains relevant whenever artificial systems are said to understand merely because they produce intelligent-looking outputs.

The deepest Searlean lesson is this: a theory of mind must not confuse the simulation of mental activity with mental activity, the description of consciousness with consciousness, the behavioral evidence for experience with experience itself, or the syntax of representation with intrinsic intentionality. Minds are not mysterious substances, but neither are they formal abstractions. They are living, conscious, intentional, embodied, biologically grounded systems whose subjective reality is part of the objective world.

PreviousDaniel Dennett NextNed Block

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
